paragraphs.text: In recent years, the Netherlands Authority for Consumers and Markets (hereinafter "ACM") researched and undertook enforcement action in the market for video games, with the aim of protecting consumers. Her knowledge and experience has been incorporated into this position paper on video games. In addition to describing the identified consumer problems, the paper also contains recommendations for European policy-making and legislation in the field of video gaming. This position paper is written as part of the ACM's wider response to the European consultation under the Digital Fairness Act, namely the ACM position paper on digital fairness and the ACM additional technical recommendations.

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: This paragraph will provide a general description of the market for video games, including recent market developments (section 2.1). Next, section 2.2 will describe how the centre of gravity of the business models in the video games industry have shifted to the in-game environment. It also elaborates on the addition of a new revenue model which monetises the time spent by consumers in video games. Finally, this section describes which consumer problems arise from these developments and why it is important for the European legislator to address them.

section_path: 2 New business models, new consumer problems

evidence_quotes.quote: There is a large variety of video games aimed at many different audiences.
the revenues from sales of subscription models

paragraphs.text: There is a large variety of video games aimed at many different audiences. In 2024 80% of the people from all generations worldwide played video games, including a large number of minors. Video games are played on so-called game consoles (e.g. PlayStation, Xbox and Nintendo), PCs and on mobile devices (e.g. mobile phones and tablets). The revenues of video games played on mobile devices account for 50% of the total revenues in the sector. Over the past 10 years, the gaming industry has grown significantly, with an average annual turnover growth of around 7.4%. In particular, during the COVID period, the sector experienced significant growth, which has slowed down somewhat thereafter. Video games generate revenue in five ways: advertising fees, revenues from selling games, revenue from in-game purchases, the revenues from sales of subscription models and revenues from user-generated content. Often video games contain multiple revenue models.

evidence_quotes.quote: publish video games built by independent developers.

paragraphs.text: There are a number of major publishers in the gaming market. Publishers issue titles sold worldwide and finance their development. Publishers often have one or more development teams (developers) in the company, and they publish video games built by independent developers. There are also smaller developers that publish video games on their own account (indies). The aforementioned console companies, PC-only platforms (e.g. Steam) and also large platforms (Apple and Google) are largely responsible for the digital distribution of video games to consumers (distributors). Digital sales represent almost the entire market for PC and mobile games, and today they also represent the largest share of the sales of console games.

evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are played for their entertainment value and as a form of leisure.
Players regularly complain about the commercialisation of video games.

paragraphs.text: Video games are played for their entertainment value and as a form of leisure. Games can also have an educational value. Video games have the ability to connect people, foster spatial understanding and encourage problem-solving and creativity. Video games are also being played competitively (e-sports). In addition to these advantages, video games also have disadvantages. Players regularly complain about the commercialisation of video games. These complaints refer to the fact that video game development often places the importance of creating turnover over the interest of consumers in playing a fun game.

paragraphs.text: However, the concerns go further. Under the pressure of competition and high consumer expectations, gaming companies are constantly looking for ways to renew games, retain players and maintain or increase turnover. Against this backdrop, video games increasingly deploy techniques (monetisation

paragraphs.text: strategies) that influence consumers' playing behaviour in order to make them spend more time and more money on video games. As will be explained in paragraph 4 of the paper, these techniques can result in different forms of consumer harm, both financial as well as social emotional and psychological harm. Consumers of video games increasingly voice their displeasure and opposition to these developments.

evidence_quotes.quote: The techniques used in video games are mapped and proposals are made for changes to consumer law that can contribute to a fairer gaming environment for consumers.

paragraphs.text: In this position paper, the ACM analyses the risks and harms caused, through the lens of European consumer law. The techniques used in video games are mapped and proposals are made for changes to consumer law that can contribute to a fairer gaming environment for consumers. It is important to note that, of course, legislative changes will not offer the entire solution. The largest and primary responsibility to provide solutions lies with the gaming industry. It needs to take the interests of society, such as choice autonomy, fairness, safety and the protection of vulnerable groups, much more seriously when developing video games. In addition, enforcement agencies, such as ACM, consumers themselves and researchers are also part of the solution.

evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are a form of entertainment for consumers, as well as a business model for gaming companies.
It is key to the business model to provide consumers with video games in which they spend their time and money and data (the in-game economy).

paragraphs.text: Video games are a form of entertainment for consumers, as well as a business model for gaming companies. It is key to the business model to provide consumers with video games in which they spend their time and money and data (the in-game economy). This paper will primarily look at the ways in which time and money are being 'earned' through video games from a consumer protection perspective. It will not focus on the business models that are deployed outside the video game, such as the purchase price and influencer marketing, or on business models that are based on data brokering.

section_path: 2.2 The in-game economy as the primary business model

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulate consumers to spend more time and money than they actually want.
video games operate fairly and grant consumers autonomy when it comes to decision making about how they spend their scarce time and money.

paragraphs.text: From a consumer perspective, it is important that video games operate fairly and grant consumers autonomy when it comes to decision making about how they spend their scarce time and money. Video games, nor any other digital service, should manipulate consumers to spend more time and money than they actually want. Also, video games should not negatively affect the well-being or health of consumers. This is particularly true for groups of consumers who are more likely to influence such as minors.

section_path: 2.2 The in-game economy as the primary business model

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game economies

paragraphs.text: The sections 2.2.1 and 2.2.2 will explain the functioning of in-game economies when it comes to 'earning' and spending money and time. It will also assess when and how these practices can be harmful to consumers.

section_path: 2.2 The in-game economy as the primary business model

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes.

paragraphs.text: From the mid 2010's onwards, the so-called freemium or free-to-play model became the most prevalent business model in video games, particularly in mobile video games. The introduction of the model lead to a leap in revenue as well as to a significant shift in the types of revenue streams in the gaming industry. Whereas revenues were previously predominantly derived from box sales and physical stores, the new revenue streams were derived from micro-transactions and loot boxes.

evidence_quotes.quote: The freemium model is a revenue model in which the (basic) game is offered free of charge or at a one-off purchase price and in which continuous revenues are generated through optional micro-transactions.
These micro-transactions will grant a consumer access to cosmetic items, convenience functions, content extensions or in-game currencies.
The items range from having a purely cosmetic function to items that directly influence progress of the gameplay (pay-to-win).

paragraphs.text: The freemium model is a revenue model in which the (basic) game is offered free of charge or at a one-off purchase price and in which continuous revenues are generated through optional micro-transactions. These micro-transactions will grant a consumer access to cosmetic items, convenience functions, content extensions or in-game currencies. The items range from having a purely cosmetic function to items that directly influence progress of the gameplay (pay-to-win). Cosmetic items are particularly popular among minors.

evidence_quotes.quote: In theory, the freemium model has removed any limits on the amount of money a consumer can spend in a video game.

paragraphs.text: In theory, the freemium model has removed any limits on the amount of money a consumer can spend in a video game. Thus, it provides an incentive for gaming companies to extent the amount of time spent by

paragraphs.text: The freemium model has led to the wide spread incorporation of micro-transactions into video games, which is reason for concern since micro-transactions have scientifically been proven to cause overspending and problematic gambling behaviour. In addition, the introduction of the freemium model has had the effect of merging economic transactions with gameplay through in-game purchases. And thirdly, the gaming industry has developed a model in which in-game purchases are almost exclusively possible through so-called paid in-game currencies. Consumers can buy paid in-game currencies using official currencies (often euro's) and subsequently spent in the video game on in-game items. The changes introduced by the freemium model and the way in which the gaming industry has implemented them have had a significant impact on consumers in video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: For example, in nearly every video game, gaming companies stipulate that consumers can buy in-game currencies in pre-determined quantities (bundles) only.
As a result, consumers are often forced to buy more in-game currencies than they need for their envisaged purchase.

The left-over in-game currencies can subsequently not be exchanged back to euro's, even in situations in which a game company decides to discontinue a video game. In those situations, consumers will simply lose their money.
Paragraph 3 of this paper will examine other techniques deployed by gaming companies to steer consumers towards making in-game purchases.
It is important to mention that many consumers find these practices pervasive and misleading (paragraph 3).

paragraphs.text: There was already an imbalance of power between gaming companies on the one hand and consumers on the other. This imbalance has been further exacerbated by the introduction of in-game purchases. A gaming company has full control over the game design and it dictates the general terms and conditions that consumers must agree to in order to play a video game. Thus, gaming companies exercise a high level of control over consumers and the ways in which they can make purchases in video games, sometimes having harmful effects. For example, in nearly every video game, gaming companies stipulate that consumers can buy in-game currencies in pre-determined quantities (bundles) only. As a result, consumers are often forced to buy more in-game currencies than they need for their envisaged purchase. The left-over in-game currencies can subsequently not be exchanged back to euro's, even in situations in which a game company decides to discontinue a video game. In those situations, consumers will simply lose their money. Paragraph 3 of this paper will examine other techniques deployed by gaming companies to steer consumers towards making in-game purchases. It is important to mention that many consumers find these practices pervasive and misleading (paragraph 3).

evidence_quotes.quote: there are misleading aspects of the use of in-game virtual currencies.
In March 2025, the CPC-network jointly drew the gaming industry's attention to this issue by providing guidance through the Key Principles on in-game Currencies.

paragraphs.text: The joint EU's consumer protection agencies (hereinafter "CPC-network") have informed the gaming industry that it fails to grant consumers the rights they have when making in-game purchases and that there are misleading aspects of the use of in-game virtual currencies. In March 2025, the CPC-network jointly drew the gaming industry's attention to this issue by providing guidance through the Key Principles on in-game Currencies. In the guidelines, the CPC-network qualified paid virtual currencies as a digital representation of value. This means that consumers have various rights when making in-game purchases with paid virtual currencies, including the right to a price in the official currency. Moreover, the ACM and the European Commission had already drawn the gaming industry's attention to this requirement in their respective guidelines in 2020 and 2022. The gaming industry did not adjust her practices in response.

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game purchases are purchases.

paragraphs.text: The publication of the Key Principles has led to conversations between the CPC-network and gaming industry during which the industry has expressed disagreement with the CPC-network interpretation that in-game purchases are purchases. According to the industry, virtual currencies are forms of digital content and their spending does not lead to a contract. Nonetheless the conversations, the gaming industry has signalled that it may be ready to address some of the problems identified by the CPC-network in the Key Principles. At the time of writing, it is not clear to what extent the industry's proposals will sufficiently solve

paragraphs.text: the problems that the industry is willing to address. It is also important to note that even if the gaming industry's proposals would solve some or all of the problems, that industry continues to fundamentally deny that consumer law applies to in-game economies. It views its proposals for change as a voluntary response to consumer issues. This is and remains deeply problematic for future of consumer protection in video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game purchases

paragraphs.text: Whatever legal interpretation will eventually prove correct and irrespective of the outcome of the conversations between the gaming industry and the CPC-network, there is no reason why consumers should not have the same rights when making in-game purchases as they have for any other distance purchases. The gaming industry confirms that this is also implicitly on this point by putting forward proposals aimed at solving certain consumer problems identified by regulators. Furthermore, the gaming industry requires legal certainty in order to make the investments needed to make the adjustments.

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game purchases

paragraphs.text: The European legislator is in a position to make clear unequivocally that consumers have the same rights when making in-game purchases as when making other online purchases. By doing so, the European legislator would unambiguously clarify that in-game economies fall within the scope of consumer law which would guarantee consumers the necessary level of protection. Finally, legal clarity might avoid potential lengthy litigation as a result of decisions by consumer protection agencies.

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game purchases in video games

paragraphs.text: An increasing number of businesses relies on the retention of consumers' attention as a way of generating revenue. When consumers spend more time in a service (i.e. a video game) they will spend more money, they will share more data and they can be shown more advertisements. Particularly with the introduction of in-game purchases in video games, this model has also been embraced by the gaming industry.

section_path: 2.2.2 The attention economy as a business model in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: In addition to generating more in-game revenue, longer playtime may also provide gaming companies with a competitive advantage. The time spent by a consumer in the video game of one game company cannot be spent in the video game of the other company. This way, a gaming company is able to convert longer playtime into a larger market share. Paragraph 4 of this paper provides an overview of the influencing techniques used in video games that may lead consumers to spend more time and even excessive time in video games.

section_path: 2.2.2 The attention economy as a business model in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: excessive time spent in video games

paragraphs.text: There is a large number of studies and reports showing that excessive time spent in video games can have harmful effects on consumers and in particular on minors. These impacts range from sleep deprivation, to mental health problems and social problems such as school drop outs. This shows the importance of limiting the time consumers spent in video games to healthy amounts. Moreover, preventing excessive playtime will also contribute to increased competition in the gaming market by facilitating easier market entry.

section_path: 2.2.2 The attention economy as a business model in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: Currently, the legal means to supervise playtime are limited. Existing consumer law is only applicable when a practice can be linked to an economic transaction between the consumer and the trader (in this case the gaming company). Often the link between the time spent and a transaction, whether in social media services or in video games, will be weak, which disincentivises enforcement.

section_path: 2.2.2 The attention economy as a business model in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: techniques used by gaming companies to steer consumers to excessive playtime.
video games.

paragraphs.text: The European legislator is in a position to bring the attention economy within the scope of consumer law, thus organising effective oversight on techniques used by gaming companies to steer consumers to excessive playtime. It is noted that the Digital Services Act (hereinafter "DSA"), provided it can address the attention economy, is likely not provide a solution because it might not apply to most video games.

paragraphs.text: Recommendation 1: consumers are entitled to a comparable level of protection for in-game purchases as for other distance purchases. Given the current misunderstanding in the gaming industry, it is important that the DFA clarifies that consumer law is applicable to in-game economies.
There are two possible ways in which the legislator could achieve an outcome:

evidence_quotes.quote: video games.
The simplest way is to require in-game purchases to be made in real money (euro's).
consumers are being misled about the nature of their in-game spending

paragraphs.text: The simplest way is to require in-game purchases to be made in real money (euro's). In doing so, all potential misunderstanding as to which consumer rights apply will be removed. Moreover, it is also no longer possible that consumers are being misled about the nature of their in-game spending as described in section 3.1. Problems related to the selling of in-game currencies in bundles and their restitution will also disappear. This solution brings clarity and reduces the need for complex rules on in-game virtual currencies that are specific to video games. This choice will require adaptations in certain (types of) games. It will end the option to use mixed in-game currencies, since there will no longer be a paid in-game currency. It might also complicate certain smaller micro-transactions (less than EUR 1 cents), which from a consumer perspective may be a positive. Finally, this choice will still require a number of consumer rights to be explicitly enshrined in the law, such as the right to a transaction overview in the video game and the prohibition of layered in-game currencies (see below).

evidence_quotes.quote: paid in-game virtual currencies

paragraphs.text: By clarifying the legal status of paid in-game virtual currencies in the law in such a way that it is clear which consumer rights apply. In addition, it will be required that at least the follow matters are codified:

Prohibit the use of layered in-game currencies;
Prohibit the use of the mixed in-game currency;
Prescribe that gaming companies have to offer consumers the option to purchase in-game virtual currencies in any amount that the choose, in addition to offering them in bundles;
Prescribe that an in-game offer, always displays price in euros at least as prominently visible as the price in game currency.
The current rules allow gaming companies to offer consumers a calculation model instead of a price.
Oblige gaming companies to refund excess paid in-game currencies upon demand by a consumer;

The current rules allow gaming companies to offer consumers a calculation model instead of a price. Delete this possibility. A calculation model does not provide consumers with sufficient understanding of the price. f) Oblige gaming companies to refund excess paid in-game currencies upon demand by a consumer;

paragraphs.text: Recommendation 2: Time, like money, is scarce. Excessive gaming has demonstrably negative effect on consumers, especially for minors (see paragraph 4). Broaden the scope of consumer law to protect consumers from harmful commercial practices aimed at optimising the time consumers spend in video games$^{17}$.

The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive's current set-up could be followed when legislating protections in terms of time. The difference is that a consumer often does not enter into an explicit transaction by which he or she 'pays' his time. The obligation of the existence of a transaction will therefore have to be dropped.

paragraphs.text: The previous paragraph sets out the reasons why the European legislator should make clear what rights consumers enjoy when making in-game purchases. In addition, reference was made to influencing techniques used by gaming companies in video games to steer consumers to spending more time and money. This paragraph describes a number of these techniques that can be commonly found in video games. For each technique, it describes how they work, what the (potential) consumer harm is and what solution is available to protect consumers. Section 3.1 will describe in-game virtual currencies, section 3.2 will describe variable reward systems and section 3.3 will describe a number of other techniques.

evidence_quotes.quote: The design of these video games is focused on making consumers spend money and time in the video game.
In-game purchases could be required to either play the game (in its entirety) (pay-to-play) or could be required make meaningful progress in a the game (pay-to-win).

paragraphs.text: Many popular video games are based on the freemium model (paragraph 2). This means that these games can be downloaded and played free of charge. The design of these video games is focused on making consumers spend money and time in the video game. In-game purchases could be required to either play the game (in its entirety) (pay-to-play) or could be required make meaningful progress in a the game (pay-to-win). They can also provide access to items that are cosmetic in nature (e.g.: skins). Cosmetic purchases are often very popular among consumers and especially among minors.

evidence_quotes.quote: with the introduction of in-game virtual currencies, the gaming industry has created an additional step for making purchases in video games.
This design choice has two harmful consequences for players described in sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.2.

paragraphs.text: In-game purchases are forms of micro-transactions, the risks of which are have been extensively described scientifically. In the vast majority of current video games consumers are only able to make in-game purchases using in-game virtual currencies. As a first step the consumer purchases the in-game currency with official currencies (often euro's), or the in-game currencies are earned by playing the video game. As a next step, the consumer will spend the in-game currency in the video game when making an in-game purchase. In other words: with the introduction of in-game virtual currencies, the gaming industry has created an additional step for making purchases in video games. As a result, consumers that want to spend money in a video game are forced to convert euro's to the specific in-game virtual currency of the game before making a purchase. This design choice has two harmful consequences for players described in sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.2.

evidence_quotes.quote: These combined design choices remove the 'pain of payment' and leads to higher spending as well as to unintended spending.
The vast majority of gaming companies display the price of an in-game purchase only in-game currency and not in euros.
When making in-game purchases, in-game currencies blur the notion that one is spending real money and how much of it.
Almost no gaming company provides players with a history of their transactions in the video game.

paragraphs.text: When making in-game purchases, in-game currencies blur the notion that one is spending real money and how much of it. The vast majority of gaming companies display the price of an in-game purchase only in-game currency and not in euros. Almost no gaming company provides players with a history of their transactions in the video game. These combined design choices remove the 'pain of payment' and leads to higher spending as well as to unintended spending. Consumers confirm this in scientific studies and also on public fora. The European consumer organisation BEUC also points to this in its report in in-game currencies.

evidence_quotes.quote: The gaming industry takes the view that in-game purchases made with in-game currencies are not transactions within the scope of existing European consumer law.
The Dutch Advertising Code Commission has also recently ruled in the highest appeal body that it considers that in-game purchases should be reflected in euro prices.

paragraphs.text: The gaming industry takes the view that in-game purchases made with in-game currencies are not transactions within the scope of existing European consumer law. This is despite the fact that the ACM and the European Commission have previously communicated the opposite position. The Dutch Advertising Code Commission has also recently ruled in the highest appeal body that it considers that in-game purchases should be reflected in euro prices. The consequence of the gaming industry's position is that it is currently denies consumers basic protections in video games. It is inexplainable why the gaming industry denies consumers those rights, while presenting the in-game transactions to consumers as 'purchases'. Moreover, it follows from the Digital Content Directive that the use of digital representations of value, such as tokens, coupons and in-game virtual currencies, should not have the effect of discriminating between different payment methods. However, the industry's position exactly results in a discrimination between the use of different payment methods and is therefore contrary to the purpose of the Directive..

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, it should be mentioned that the gaming industry claims that it has based its model of in-game purchases and in-game virtual currencies on a legal position of the CPC-network which, according to in the industry, demands that gameplay should remain strictly separated from purchases. This is incorrect. First of all, the CPC-network have never adopted such a position. To the contrary, both the ACM and the European Commission have indicated in their respective guidelines that they require, inter alia, a price in euros for in-game purchases, which implies that they consider in-game purchases as purchases. Secondly, the industry's claim is relies on a reference to an older guidance document produced by the UK regulator, which was based on a gaming market that was different from the current one. And lastly, it is important to note that by adopting the freemium model, the gaming industry is responsible for making economic transactions an integral part of gameplay. This economic reality cannot be denied by refusing to acknowledge that in-game purchases are purchases in the legal sense, as the industry currently attempts to do.

paragraphs.text: It is not clear in advance what rewards the consumer will receive from a variable reward system; the content is the result of a lottery draw. The winning probabilities of the different rewards are determined by the gaming company. As in the case of 'ordinary' lotteries, a variable reward system usually works in such a way that the chance of obtaining more valuable items is many times lower than the chance of winning less valuable items. The following paragraphs refer to loot boxes and variable reward systems interchangeably, because the scientific literature is not consistent in its use of terminology. However, what is described below applies to all variable reward systems.

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes in video games.

paragraphs.text: Scientists and researchers point out that variable reward systems have many similarities with gambling and, as such, also present similar risks as gambling practices do. People buying variable reward systems are more likely to experience excessive game-behaviour and gambling addiction. In its 2024 Fitness Check on Digital Fairness report, the European Commission also highlights the risks of loot boxes in video games. In addition, the Norwegian Consumer Council also issued a report in 2022 describing these risks. As early as 2020, the risks, in particular those for minors, were explained in detail in a report prepared by the European Parliament. In the light of similar concerns, in early 2025 the US Federal Trade Commission prohibited the game company behind the video game Genshin Impact from offering loot boxes to children under 16, unless parents give their consent.

evidence_quotes.quote: between gaming companies and consumer enforcement agencies.

paragraphs.text: Up to date, European consumer law does not contain any specific provisions addressing variable reward systems. The question whether the principle-based standards of European consumer law apply to variable reward systems might become the subject of legal proceedings between gaming companies and consumer enforcement agencies. In any case, without a general prohibition of those systems, current consumer law will require enforcement agencies to assess each variable reward system against the law individually. This situation creates legal uncertainty for both businesses and consumers and it puts a substantial work load on the competent authorities. Furthermore, it leads to relatively high compliance and enforcement costs. An explicit ban on variable reward systems would provide clarity to all parties involved and it would ensure the necessary protection for consumers.
Recommendation 3: prohibit variable reward systems in video games, including loot boxes, at a European level.

evidence_quotes.quote: variable reward systems in video games

paragraphs.text: In its response to the EC Fitness Check on Digital Fairness in 2022, the ACM formulated the same recommendation. Almost three years later, the picture of the harms and risks of variable reward systems in video games has only become clearer. The harms of variable reward systems do not just consist of overspending, but also of increased risks of gambling addiction and excessive gambling behaviour. There is a broad body of scientific research that shows that consumers are widely affected by variable reward systems, not just more vulnerable groups.

Therefore, the ACM calls for a ban on the use of variable reward systems in video games.

Furthermore, general scientific research on the effects of transparency requirements shows that these requirements often have no or limited positive impact on the consumer behaviour they seek to change. There is no reasons to assume that the situation will be better regarding transparency requirements related to variable reward systems. Therefore, the ACM calls for a ban on the use of variable reward systems in video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: The gaming industry shows creativity when it comes to developing and deploying influencing techniques in video games (monetisation strategies).

paragraphs.text: The gaming industry shows creativity when it comes to developing and deploying influencing techniques in video games (monetisation strategies). In-game virtual currencies and variable reward systems can serve as examples of that creativity. Some of the influencing techniques that saw first use in video games find their way to other sectors such as regular e-commerce. There is nothing wrong with these development as long as the influencing techniques do not mislead consumers, do not cause harm and remain within the framework of the law. However, such techniques are not always benign and harmless. This section will provide an overview of the various techniques that are being used and how they influence consumer behaviour.

section_path: 3.3 Other influencing techniques in video games

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns are influencing techniques that mislead consumers and manipulate them to making purchases that they would not have made otherwise.
dark patterns are widely present in video games.

paragraphs.text: Gaming companies regularly use the same dark patterns as other digital actors such as social media platforms or businesses in e-commerce. Dark patterns are influencing techniques that mislead consumers and manipulate them to making purchases that they would not have made otherwise. Examples are the use of peer pressure to make minors make purchases or misleading count down clocks. In 2024, the ACM fined the gaming company Epic Games for using misleading count down timers in its video game Fortnite. Furthermore, a recent sweep by the International Consumer Protection and Enforcement Network (ICPEN) shows that dark patterns are widely present in video games.
Authority for Consumers and Markets
Public

evidence_quotes.quote: (monetary dark patterns)
(temporal dark patterns)
social capital dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Research shows that video games feature influencing techniques that are (more or less) unique to the game environment. Despite the fact that gaming companies do not provide access to their user and test data, scientific research clearly shows that some of these influencing techniques deployed in video games manipulate consumers and mislead them to spend money (monetary dark patterns), encourage consumers to spend more time in video games (temporal dark patterns), and deploy forms of social pressures on consumers through social capital dark patterns. In addition, reference is made to a number of lawsuits that have been recently initiated in the United States in which game companies are accused of deliberately incorporating addictive techniques into video games. Finally, there is a website with a large overview of dark patterns used in video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: influencing techniques used in video games that are specific to gaming.

paragraphs.text: By way of illustration, this paper will provide a non-exhaustive overview of some of the influencing techniques used in video games that are specific to gaming. These techniques are not necessarily misleading or harmful. However they can be in specific circumstances, when deployed jointly, when used in combination with pay-to-win business models, or when used in relation to minors:

evidence_quotes.quote: In this way, a lock-in is created and then exploited.

paragraphs.text: Lock-in: at the start of the game, many video games offer consumers reasonably smooth progress in the game against low costs (or even against rewards) and low time use (priming). As the game progresses and consumers become more locked-in (sunk cost), the speed of the progress in the game decreases and the pressure to make purchases increases. In this way, a lock-in is created and then exploited.

evidence_quotes.quote: Power curve or power creep: through this technique, a gaming company ensures a continuous introduction of new, stronger elements of the game, thus reducing the relative value of previous game elements.

paragraphs.text: Power curve or power creep: through this technique, a gaming company ensures a continuous introduction of new, stronger elements of the game, thus reducing the relative value of previous game elements. This requires consumers to continuously invest time and/or money in the game to be able to obtain the newer elements in order to compete or progress in the video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: Grinding: using this technique, a gaming company introduces a delay or obstruction in the progress of the game, which may consist, for example, of the need to perform repetitive actions.
The consumer is then given the opportunity to overcome the obstruction or speed up the game progress by making purchases.

paragraphs.text: Grinding: using this technique, a gaming company introduces a delay or obstruction in the progress of the game, which may consist, for example, of the need to perform repetitive actions. The consumer is then given the opportunity to overcome the obstruction or speed up the game progress by making purchases.

evidence_quotes.quote: in the video game in which certain rewards can be earned, but which are only available on a temporary basis.
This could lead to excess playtime or spending in order to win the presented rewards.
Those rewards are continuously shown, even if they are not yet earned.

paragraphs.text: Battle pass: buying a so-called battle pass will grant a consumer access to a process in the video game in which certain rewards can be earned, but which are only available on a temporary basis. Those rewards are continuously shown, even if they are not yet earned. In advance, it is often unclear what investment is required in terms of time and/or money to earn the rewards presented. As consumers are more advanced in the process and have invested more, they will be less likely to give up (lock-in). This could lead to excess playtime or spending in order to win the presented rewards.

paragraphs.text: Pay-to-keep or pay-to-revive: using this technique, a gaming company will require a consumer to make a payment to keep a game character or a game item that he/she already possesses, or to revive a game character or item that has just been lost (loss aversion and emotional attachment).

paragraphs.text: Scarcity of in-game items: scarcity cues are well-known techniques to encourage consumers to make purchases. However, this technique has new dimensions when it comes to creating scarcity of digital items. A gaming company has a large degree of freedom to determine the number of in-game items it offers. By definition, digital items are not scarce because they can be 'added' at almost zero costs. It could be argued that any scarcity created for digital in-game items is always artificial and, therefore, referencing that scarcity in order to pressure consumers is by definition misleading.

evidence_quotes.quote: could be adjusted in such a way that a consumer is steered towards making purchases in order to increase the chances of winning
This type of personalised manipulation can be expected to be particularly effective.
There is a third category of influencing techniques that are potentially being deployed in video games but that are difficult to detect using the current consumer law powers.

paragraphs.text: There is a third category of influencing techniques that are potentially being deployed in video games but that are difficult to detect using the current consumer law powers. If deployed they have the potential of being highly manipulative. As an example, matchmaking is taken here. Many video games have a competitive game element in which consumers compete with one another or with the computer. The video game, by means of an AI, determines the strength of the opponent, whether human or bot. The video game will offer no transparency on how the video game selects an opponent. The matchmaking process or the competitive event could be adjusted in such a way that a consumer is steered towards making purchases in order to increase the chances of winning (match fixing, monetised matchmaking and dynamic difficulty adjustment). At that moment, the video game could offer the consumer a discount on a purchase that will facilitate the progress in the game, for example under time pressure. This type of personalised manipulation can be expected to be particularly effective. It is unclear whether it is currently used in video games. In any event, certain gaming companies possess patents on some of these techniques.

However, at the same time, on the basis of behavioural insights and research, it is clear that some of those same game elements steer consumers towards spending more time and money than they otherwise would have, this creating harms. And precisely the fact that these influence techniques form such an integral part of the game experience is what makes them so effective and potentially more harmful

paragraphs.text: This paragraph will describe the reasons why minors need better protections in video games (section 4.1) also against advertising practices (section 4.2). It emphasises the responsibility of the gaming industry to design games that are fair and safe for minors and other vulnerable groups (section 4.5) and what role age verification tools (section 4.3) and parental controls (section 4.4) can play in providing safety and fairness for minors in video games.

section_path: 4 The protection of minors in video games

paragraphs.text: It is a widely accepted fact in psychological science that the executive functions of minors are less well developed than those of adults. As a result, minors are particularly sensitive to (certain) influencing techniques. As explained in several sections of this paper, exposure to these techniques can negatively affect the mental and emotional development of minors. Such risks should be prevented even if the effects have not yet been sufficiently proven.

evidence_quotes.quote: It is also clear that many video games played by minors contain dark patterns.
the majority of video games directly encourage minors to make purchases

paragraphs.text: Existing European consumer law already contains a general legal obligation to protect minors. However, it is clear that many gaming companies do not take this obligation sufficiently seriously. Recent research and enforcement actions show that the majority of video games directly encourage minors to make purchases, whereas this has been explicitly prohibited since the introduction of European consumer law 20 years ago. It is also clear that many video games played by minors contain dark patterns. Game-companies are fully aware that their video games are played by minors. Despite this knowledge, it is only recently that the first signs can be observed that gaming companies are introducing measures that protect minors without fully placing the responsibility for the protection of minors on parents or carers (sections 4.3 and 4.4).

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game influencing techniques such as variable reward systems

paragraphs.text: Based on the current level of scientific knowledge the European legislator could increase the protection of minors by introducing bans on in-game influencing techniques such as variable reward systems, in-game virtual currencies and some specific influencing techniques in video games such as monetised matchmaking, time pressure, emotional pressure or battle passes.
Authority for Consumers and Markets
Public

evidence_quotes.quote: Many video games are (partly) financed by revenue earned through advertising in the video game.

paragraphs.text: Many video games are (partly) financed by revenue earned through advertising in the video game (see paragraph 2). In-game advertising takes many different forms. Commercials can be clearly marked and delineated from the gameplay. However, video games, like other environments, also include forms of hidden advertising. For example, products or brand names can be visible in the gameplay (product placement) but not clearly marked as advertising. Another variant is that brands advertise in proto-metaverse games, in which they open shops that can be visited by consumers and in which physical or digital branded products are for sale.

evidence_quotes.quote: Increasingly, so called "cross overs" are being launched in video games.
In-game Non-Player Characters (NPC's) could be promoting products or services, without it being clear that it is an NPC and not a human being.
An example which is regularly mentioned in the literature is a digital variant of door-to-door canvassing.

paragraphs.text: Increasingly, so called "cross overs" are being launched in video games. These are advertising cooperations between producers, brand owners, advertisers and a video gaming companies. An example would be the appearance of skins from Marvel-figures in a video game around the launch date of a new Marvel-film. With the growth of proto-metaverses, there will also be more new advertising opportunities, some yet beyond our current imagination.78 An example which is regularly mentioned in the literature is a digital variant of door-to-door canvassing. In-game Non-Player Characters (NPC's) could be promoting products or services, without it being clear that it is an NPC and not a human being. Research has clearly shown that minors do not recognise many of the forms of advertising described here.

evidence_quotes.quote: video games played by minors contain advertisements that are unsuitable for minors.

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, despite the existing obligations of the Audiovisual Media Services Directive, some video games played by minors contain advertisements that are unsuitable for minors. Often, those advertisements do also not meet the requirements as laid down by Dutch law or by the Dutch Advertising Code for Children79.

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: To conclude: advertising and marketing practices in video games still present many challenges in terms of protecting minors, with new challenges on the way. By tightening and clarifying the rules, the European legislator could increase the protection of minors in video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
video games

paragraphs.text: One way to protect minors from harmful commercial practices in video games, such as variable reward systems, dark patterns and inappropriate advertisements, is by denying or restricting their access to these video games. This can be done through age verification systems or age-gates. Currently, when using age verification, the gaming industry deploys age verification systems that function based on self-reporting. Consumers are demanded to declare their own age. On the basis of that information, it is determined whether a consumer meets the age requirement for the video game and whether the player is admitted to the video game or not. These forms of age verification do not provide sufficient guarantees that minors do not play video games that are unsuitable for them.

section_path: 4.3 Age verification tools are not the holy grail

evidence_quotes.quote: minors have access to video games that are not suitable for them.

paragraphs.text: It is well known that self-reporting age verification systems are easy to circumvent and that this happens a lot in practice. As a result, minors have access to video games that are not suitable for them. Age verification systems with a higher level of reliability are being developed80. However, It is still unclear when the proposed solutions could be implemented. In the meantime, gaming companies could and should do more to better protect minors in their video games.

section_path: 4.3 Age verification tools are not the holy grail

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair influencing techniques

paragraphs.text: It is also important to note that the introduction of robust age verification systems for video games, although important, is not in itself a guarantee that the (parts of) video games to which minors have access, are suitable for minors. Firstly, because even the most advanced age verification systems are not completely watertight81. But more importantly because a robust age check will not guarantee that a video game will be void of features that are harmful to minors like unfair influencing techniques or unsuitable advertisements. In other words: as important a tool age verification can be, it does not dismiss gaming companies from the responsibility to design fair video games (section 4.1).

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: Many gaming companies offer parents and/or carers the possibility to change certain settings in video games in order to limit some actions by minors, for example making purchases or playing beyond a certain amount of hours. The gaming industry takes the position that, by offering these parental controls and running information campaigns for parents, it fulfils its responsibilities to protect minors. However, in taking this position the industry largely ignores that legally the primary responsibility to protect minors in video games, rests on gaming companies. They could meet that responsibility either by designing video games that are suitable for minors or by effectively denying minors access to games that are not suitable to them.

section_path: 4.4 Parental controls should not be the first line of defence

evidence_quotes.quote: many video games actually actively encourage spending by minors through direct exhortations and marketing campaigns

paragraphs.text: Gaming companies are well aware of the fact that their video games are being played by minors and that age verification tools are easily and often circumvented. Yet, many video games actually actively encourage spending by minors through direct exhortations and marketing campaigns that, if not targeting minors directly inside and outside the video game, are at least appealing to minors. In doing so, in effect, the gaming industry reverses the order of responsibilities by making parents and carers primarily responsible to protect minors, while at the same time deploying practices to encourage minors to spend money and time in their video games.

section_path: 4.4 Parental controls should not be the first line of defence

evidence_quotes.quote: For example, a setting could allow of the limitation of playtime for over 10 hours, but not for a self-selected amount of time.
Or a video game may allow a parent and/or carer to turn off the option for in-game purchases, but it will not allow to turn off the existence of unpaid variable rewards systems.

paragraphs.text: Although parental controls do have a place in the protection of minors, their effectiveness should not be overestimated. Firstly, the settings that can be toggled through parental controls or often limited. For example, a setting could allow of the limitation of playtime for over 10 hours, but not for a self-selected amount of time. Or a video game may allow a parent and/or carer to turn off the option for in-game purchases, but it will not allow to turn off the existence of unpaid variable rewards systems.

section_path: 4.4 Parental controls should not be the first line of defence

evidence_quotes.quote: A significant group of parents and/or carers do not have a clear picture of the risks in video games or are not aware of or able to toggle the setting.
parents or carers may not be aware of or have access to all the accounts created by a minor on an online platforms.

paragraphs.text: Secondly, it should be noted that researchers indicate that, for several reasons, it is unrealistic and undesirable to place the full responsibility for the protection of minors on parents and carers. A significant group of parents and/or carers do not have a clear picture of the risks in video games or are not aware of or able to toggle the setting. Additionally, parents or carers may not be aware of or have access to all the accounts created by a minor on an online platforms. Using multiple accounts is a common practice on many online platforms and this can reduce the mitigating effect of the role of parents and/or carers. It is also known that minors with a poor relationship with their educators, or who are, for example, orphans, are at the highest risk online. In those circumstances parental setting will have limited effect in protecting minors.

section_path: 4.4 Parental controls should not be the first line of defence

evidence_quotes.quote: Video games that are accessible to minors – currently nearly all video games – should be designed fairly and in compliance with the law, offering maximum protections to minors.
opt-out, rather on an opt-in, allowing certain potentially harmful game features that are turned off by default.

paragraphs.text: The ACM proposes a different approach to parental controls. Video games that are accessible to minors – currently nearly all video games – should be designed fairly and in compliance with the law, offering maximum protections to minors. Parental controls should not operate as a first line of defence. They should be used for parents and/or carers to be able to opt-out, rather on an opt-in, allowing certain potentially harmful game features that are turned off by default.

section_path: 4.4 Parental controls should not be the first line of defence

paragraphs.text: Besides minors, there are other vulnerable groups that the gaming industry could better protect. This includes: people with psychiatric disorders, people with lower cognitive abilities, elderly people and impulse buyers, analphabetic people, and neurodivergent people. paragraphs.text: Recommendation 8: create the following EU bans for video games that are or can be played by minors:
A prohibition on displaying advertisements that are unsuitable for minors;
A prohibition on the display of advertisements which are not recognisable as such by minors;

paragraphs.text: Ban on the use of variable reward systems, paid in-game currencies and harmful in-game influence techniques in relation to minors;
Ban personalised in-game advertising and influence techniques aimed at minors.
Based on the precautionary principle these prohibitions should be introduced, even if for some aspects the immediate harm to minors has not been extensively research and proven scientifically.

evidence_quotes.quote: minors are part of the customer base of (gaming) companies.

paragraphs.text: Recommendation 9: create the legal possibility to apply the open standards of consumer law without the necessary transactional test or with a presumption of proof when minors are part of the customer base of (gaming) companies.

Current consumer law requires traders, including gaming companies, to provide additional protections to vulnerable target groups, including minors (section 4.1 and 4.5). It is often difficult for enforcement agencies to demonstrate that this responsibility is upheld and that as a result the behaviour of minors is negatively affected.

Partly, this is due to the fact that in science there are many additional requirements for research among children. As a result, empirical evidence is not readily available and costly to produce. It is also difficult to produce empirical evidence that establishes a causal link between children's behaviour and certain commercial practices, because there are so many different parameters involved.

evidence_quotes.quote: statistics produced by gaming companies
the behaviour of minors in the video game.

paragraphs.text: Moreover, due to the use of weak age verification tools, statistics produced by gaming companies do often not provide sufficiently reliable information on the behaviour of minors in the video game. Provided enforcement agencies receive the information from a gaming company, it is likely provide an insufficient basis for a proper analysis and follow-up intervention.

evidence_quotes.quote: drop the requirement of the transactional test in relation to violations against minors
introduce a presumption of proof concerning the effects on minors

paragraphs.text: In practice, this means that despite the existence of a high legal standard for the protection of minors, it is not easy to investigate and enforce the standard. In order to facilitate effective enforcement, the ACM proposes to either drop the requirement of the transactional test in relation to violations against minors or to introduce a presumption of proof concerning the effects on minors, which could be refuted by the trader (also see recommendation 6). Such a legislative change could be justified by a reference to the precautionary principle of the International Convention on the Rights of the Child (section 5.3).

paragraphs.text: The last paragraph of this position paper is dedicated a number of topics related to the more technical side of creating legislation. Section 5.1 will go into the question why legislative changes are need in relation to video games. The sections 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4 will respectively elaborate on how to incorporate the new rules in the existing legal consumer protection framework, when to make normative or evidence-based rules and on how to guarantee convergence in norms across the EU/EEA.

evidence_quotes.quote: video games use commercial practices that harm consumers and minors in particular.

paragraphs.text: Self-regulation and supervisory interventions have so far not been sufficiently successful in encouraging the gaming industry to ensure broad compliance with consumer law. In the meantime, video games use commercial practices that harm consumers and minors in particular. The industry indicates that it considers the current legal rules insufficiently clear and takes this as a reason for slow and partly compliance with the rules. This calls for a strengthening and clarification of the rules as well as for more enforcement. If the rules are to be effective in terms of compliance, they should preferably be established at a European level.

evidence_quotes.quote: the gaming sector does not feel sufficiently urged to comply with existing consumer law.

paragraphs.text: It is clear that, despite previous supervisory interventions and existing codes of conduct, the gaming sector does not feel sufficiently urged to comply with existing consumer law. This is particularly true for the larger gaming providers. Consumers are harmed as a result. Education initiatives by the industry do not materially change the situation (section 4.4). The primary cause of the problem is not the behaviour of consumers but the behaviour of the gaming sector. Consumer knowledge can contribute to a solution, but the core of the solution cannot and should not be sought on the side of consumers. These conclusions are shared by scientists $^{90}$ .

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: Discussions with industry experts make it clear that within the gaming sector, incorrectly, is under the impression that existing consumer law does either not apply to video games, or only partly. The impression seems change slowly, however this is not yet visibly leading to broad compliance with the law. With explicit reference to the lack of clarity of certain legal standards, the industry actively resists certain changes that are clearly in the interest of their consumers. Against this background, it is obvious that the legislator should intervene and provide the necessary clarity on standards on a number of important issues as described in this paper.

evidence_quotes.quote: The ACM is convinced that the gaming industry would also benefit from clearer rules.

paragraphs.text: The ACM is convinced that the gaming industry would also benefit from clearer rules. Firstly, clear rules better ensure a level-playing field for gaming companies, including in relation to those operating from outside the EU/EEA, as consumer law applies to any company operating on the European market, irrespective of its place of establishment.

evidence_quotes.quote: the gaming industry states that some of its chain partners $^{91}$ , which are not themselves subject to consumer law, make compliance with the rules practically difficult or impossible.

paragraphs.text: In addition, clear standards also create legal certainty for businesses. Legal certainty creates cost savings for the industry in terms of compliance and litigation costs. Furthermore, the gaming industry states that some of its chain partners $^{91}$ , which are not themselves subject to consumer law, make compliance with the rules practically difficult or impossible. Clear legal standards may convince chain partners of the need to cooperate.

As a result many provisions, such as those on the protection of minors (art 28 DSA) or the prohibition on dark patterns (art 25 DSA), may not be applicable to the majority of video games.

evidence_quotes.quote: prohibitions of variable reward systems and paid layered in-game currencies
the obligation to provide overviews of historic transactions
the obligation to provide access to data for research purposes

paragraphs.text: The proposed changes to the rules on video games as described in this position paper can, according to the ACM, largely fit into the current system of consumer law. For example, prohibitions of variable reward systems and paid layered in-game currencies can be designed as additions to the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive black list. This will limit the need for sector specific rules as much as possible. However, a number of provisions are specific to practices in video games and may require specific provisions. For example, the obligation to provide overviews of historic transactions (recommendation 1) or the obligation to provide access to data for research purposes (recommendation 5).

In particular in economic law, it is often preferable for rules to be established based on scientific evidence (evidence-based). The European Commission asserts that this should also serve as the foundation for the legislative effort on the Digital Fairness Act. The ACM supports this approach. Therefore, this position paper presents empirical evidence for the problems identified.

However, there may be valid reasons to deviate from this principle. One important reason lies in the precautionary principle. This principle is grounded in the idea that policies and legislation are designed to prevent or mitigate major risks, especially when research on those risks and harms is inconclusive or when conducting research is challenging.

This principle is enshrined in the EU Treaty concerning to environmental protection rules and is applied in various European directives on chemicals, food safety and public health. In addition, the precautionary principle is also enshrined in the International Convention on the Rights of the Child. The UN Commission on the Rights of the Child explicitly notes that many digital technologies were not developed with consideration for their use by minors. At the same time, these technologies are highly appealing to minors and have a significant impact on their lives.

evidence_quotes.quote: There are reasons to apply the precautionary principle when formulating regulations on video games, particularly with regard to the protection of minors (paragraph 4).

paragraphs.text: There are reasons to apply the precautionary principle when formulating regulations on video games, particularly with regard to the protection of minors (paragraph 4). Firstly, new influencing techniques are constantly being developed and deployed in video games. It is impossible to continuously and promptly produce scientific evidence on the effects of these techniques to base new regulatory or enforcement decisions on. Moreover, the reluctance of the gaming industry to share data that could support research significantly limits the scope of meaningful studies that can be conducted (recommendation 5).

evidence_quotes.quote: minors, as a group, are especially vulnerable to the effects of influencing techniques

paragraphs.text: Moreover, with regard to minors, scientific research is both complex and costly to conduct, while financial resources are limited. Finally, and most importantly, it is unacceptable to wait for potential negative health effects especially those with irreversible consequences to materialize before implementing protective measures. This is particularly true given that it has already been established that minors, as a group, are especially vulnerable to the effects of influencing techniques (paragraph 4).

evidence_quotes.quote: regulatory frameworks for videogames to ensure a high level of protection for minors.

paragraphs.text: These circumstances justify the application of the precautionary principle and the establishment of regulatory frameworks for videogames to ensure a high level of protection for minors.
Authority for Consumers and Markets
Public

evidence_quotes.quote: Video games are generally consistent across all EU member states.

paragraphs.text: Video games are generally consistent across all EU member states. The largest game-providers (publishers) are multinational companies, typically based outside the EU/EEA. They treat the European market as a unified whole and often disregard rules established by individual member states. This is evident in the example of the national ban on loot boxes in Belgium, which has resulted in relatively low compliance levels across the gaming industry[87].

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game virtual currencies and loot boxes

paragraphs.text: A similar pattern of low compliance levels emerges with regard to the requirements outlined in the ACM's Guidelines on Protection of the Online Consumer in relation to in-game virtual currencies and loot boxes (paragraph 3). These examples demonstrate the need for rules to be established at the European level and for these rules to apply uniformly across the entire EU/EEA. In other words, there is a need for maximum harmonization. This benefits both gaming companies operating across borders and consumer protection enforcement agencies collaborating internationally.

evidence_quotes.quote: Dutch Loot Box and Video Game Consumer Protection Guidelines.
